authoritative_sources.title: The Emergency Alert System (EAS) TV Handbook
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FCC is the authoritative U.S. broadcast regulator. The EAS TV Handbook defines required operational handling and testing behavior that scheduling cannot override for linear broadcast services.
used_for: authority boundaries for on-air operational tests

authoritative_sources.title: Public Inspection Files
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FCC public inspection files are the authoritative public record system for licensed U.S. broadcast stations and establish the broadcast-specific recordkeeping context that generic scheduling does not capture.

authoritative_sources.title: Closed Captioning of Internet Protocol-Delivered Video Programming
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FCC order implementing CVAA captioning obligations is the primary U.S. public baseline for scheduling internet-delivered video only when caption ownership and readiness responsibilities are satisfied.

authoritative_sources.title: Basic Agreement of 2023 - Article 7 Directors' Minimum Conditions Preparation, Production and Post-Production
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The DGA agreement is a recognized controlling industry authority for production scheduling changes, director consultation, and AD or UPM relationships on covered motion picture and television work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Production schedule governance and approval boundaries
used_for: adjacent authority mapping to production management
refusal of final greenlight or budget authority

authoritative_sources.title: Ready, Tech, Go: Digital Tools for the 2nd AD
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The DGA AD/UPM council is a recognized professional workflow authority, and this public event summary explicitly ties 2nd AD work to callsheets, production reports, script breakdowns, and scheduling tool usage.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Shoot-day planning and production update packaging
used_for: call sheet and production report artifacts

authoritative_sources.title: California Code of Regulations, Title 8, Section 11760. Working Hours of Minors
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: California DIR publishes a controlling labor rule widely used in U.S. film and television production for minor work-hour, schooling, and rest limits that materially constrain feasible schedules.
used_for: escalation when shoot-day timing conflicts with protected limits

authoritative_sources.title: Ontology for Media Creation, Part 2: Context
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: MovieLabs is a recognized studio-backed industry consortium, and its ontology defines shoot-day context, production units, locations, and schedule-change upkeep used in film and streaming production workflows.
used_for: systems and artifact naming for shoot-day context

authoritative_sources.title: SMPTE OV 2021-0:2020 Broadcast Exchange Format - Roadmap for the 2021 Document Suite
authoritative_sources.publisher: Society of Motion Picture and Television Engineers
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SMPTE is the principal recognized standards body for broadcast engineering, and the BXF roadmap explicitly covers schedule, as-run, playlist-update, and acquisition-failure message flows between traffic and automation systems.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Traffic, automation, and as-run schedule exchange
used_for: systems-of-record mapping for schedule and as-run evidence

authoritative_sources.title: AMWA AS-03 MXF Program Delivery Specification
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AMWA is a recognized broadcast workflow standards body, and AS-03 provides vendor-neutral delivery-package expectations that determine whether a scheduled release or transmission handoff is actually ready.
used_for: refusal when package readiness is incomplete

workflow_stages.name: Demand intake and schedule-frame normalization
workflow_stages.description: Classify incoming requests, confirm that the work is really broadcast scheduling, and normalize rundown, traffic, shoot-day, booking, rights, and delivery context into one planning frame.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Constraint and capacity assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess crew, stage, control-room, edit, truck, transmission-window, turnaround, accessibility, and rights constraints before recommending any timing or allocation change.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Schedule, sequence, and booking recommendation support
workflow_stages.description: Generate recommendation-only sequencing, deferment, fallback-window, and owner-readiness outputs using approved tie-break rules and named industry records.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Delivery, accessibility, and on-air or publish readiness gating
workflow_stages.description: Determine whether a slot, shoot day, transmission handoff, or stream publish time can be recommended based on caption, delivery-manifest, public broadcast, and playout-readiness evidence.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Refresh, late-change, and adjacent-authority escalation
workflow_stages.description: Withdraw or revise tentative commitments when acquisition failures, rights conflicts, labor violations, or approval-boundary issues emerge and route to the correct owner.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: rundowns, traffic logs, break clocks, playlists, as-run records, house numbers, Ad-ID references, and EIDR references
shooting schedules, strip boards, day-out-of-days, call sheets, production reports, and script breakdowns
studio, control-room, truck, satellite, fiber, and edit-bay booking records
rights windows, embargo notes, contract availability records, blackout restrictions, and advertiser or sponsor commitments
caption, subtitle, audio-description, transcript, QC, timecode, and delivery-manifest packages
FCC public-file records, EAS operational materials, and accessibility-ownership notes when applicable
constraint logs, approval-needed summaries, readiness summaries, and escalation packets
systems_of_record: tenant approval matrices and policy libraries

allowed: Classify scheduling work as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Assess whether available records support a bounded schedule, routing, booking, or allocation recommendation.
Produce evidence-backed schedule recommendations, readiness summaries, constraint logs, and escalation packages without claiming final approval authority.
escalate: Required rundown, call-sheet, rights, caption, delivery, public-record, or tenant-policy records are missing or materially conflict.
A labor-rule, minor-performer, accessibility, broadcast-operations, or rights condition changes whether work may proceed.
The request requires final programming, legal, editorial, master-control, engineering, labor-interpretation, or financial approval authority.
Public baseline guidance and tenant policy materially conflict on release readiness, schedule precedence, or compliance handling.
refuse: Commit final air, publish, release, or shoot approval.
Approve rights availability, legal waivers, union or child-labor interpretation, caption waivers, or engineering release.
Treat generic calendars or project plans as sufficient substitutes for named broadcast, production, or delivery records.

domain_failure_modes: booking linear airtime or a stream launch without confirmed rights, captions, or delivery readiness
committing a shoot day without validated call-sheet, turnaround, minor-schooling, or production-unit constraints
treating rundown or traffic revisions as optional when they are the source of truth for timing and as-run accountability
allowing schedule pressure to override accessibility, public broadcast, labor, or rights blockers
promising fallback windows without checking facility, control-room, ingest, or acquisition-failure recovery capacity
collapsing the lane into generic project scheduling without roundups of house numbers, delivery manifests, rights windows, or public compliance hooks
acting as legal, editorial, master-control, or final programming authority instead of escalating to the correct owner

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Demand exceeds capacity or approved service levels
Conflicting priorities with no approved tie-break rule
Stakeholder asks for final approval the lane cannot grant
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Collective bargaining terms, turnaround rules, travel rules, and minor-performer limits vary materially by union agreement, jurisdiction, and production type.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The public baseline establishes that these rules can change schedule feasibility, but runtime must still retrieve tenant-specific agreements and location context before deterministic labor-sensitive recommendations.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Broadcast-only obligations such as EAS handling and public-file posture do not apply uniformly to every film or streaming-only tenant.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: This is handled by making service type, license posture, and platform context mandatory runtime facts before invoking broadcast-specific record or operational checks.

notes: The research baseline intentionally covers workflow, evidence, terminology, systems, and refusal behavior instead of generic scheduling mechanics only.

The lane remains recommendation-only even when it handles complex timing, routing, or fallback planning; final programming, legal, compliance, labor, and engineering authorities stay outside the lane.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for validated use because all known variation is explicitly managed through runtime retrieval and escalation requirements.
